slides.title:
A Rosetta Stone
for your data
slides.subtitle: A governed semantic model lets an LLM answer plain-English questions in SQL — the way the stone let scholars read a lost language through one they already knew.
slides.caption: The Rosetta Stone — photo by Hans Hillewaert, licensed CC BY-SA 4.0 (Wikimedia Commons).

slides.title: The problem with pointing an LLM at your database
slides.caption: Our engine still lets the model write SQL — but over a virtual table of your governed metrics and dimensions, and every query is parsed and validated before it can run.

slides.title: The pipeline: question → validated SQL → answer

rows.title: Planner — LLM writes semantic SQL
rows.subtitle: SELECT over ONE virtual table (metrics + dimensions)

rows.title: Parse + Validate
rows.subtitle: sqlglot + model check — the safety boundary

slides.caption: The LLM authors SQL, but only over a virtual model table; parse + validate is the boundary. From the IR down it's pure, tested code, so joins and aggregation can't drift or double-count.

slides.title: One question, end to end
slides.caption: A single turn as a sequence: the LLM lane PROPOSES (rewrite → plan → summarize) and the deterministic engine lane DISPOSES (validate → compile → run). Parse+validate is the safety boundary; on a recoverable error the planner re-plans (bounded repair). 2 LLM calls on a fresh question, 3 on a follow-up.

slides.title: The system prompt (built from the YAML model)
slides.code:
You translate natural-language questions into SQL over a semantic layer.
Write ONE SQL SELECT against a single virtual table named `product_sales`.
Its only columns are the dimensions and metrics listed below. Metrics are already-aggregated measures — select them by name; do NOT wrap them in SUM/COUNT/etc. Never join, never reference physical tables or columns, and never use SELECT *.

ABOUT THE DATA (background only — you still write ONE SELECT over `product_sales`; the engine resolves any joins for you):
- fact_sales — Combined in-store (NCR/POS) and online (OLO) product-level transaction detail. One order has many rows. (grain: one row per product line per transaction)
- dim_store — Store/franchise master: one row per store, with geography, ownership type, and lifecycle status.
- fact_budget — The company's sales goals — planned/target net sales per store per day. (Physical table: fact_budget.) (grain: one row per store per calendar day)

DIMENSIONS (group-by / filter columns):
- product_name  [synonyms: item, product, sku name; examples: Cappuccino, Americano, Black Coffee]
- category_name  [synonyms: category, product category, item category; examples: Espresso Drinks, Coffee, Pastries]
- purchase_location  [synonyms: channel, sales channel, in-store vs online, where purchased; examples: At Shop, Online]
- date  [synonyms: business date, transaction date, day]
- month — Calendar month (one bucket per month per year).  [synonyms: month, calendar month, year month; examples: 2026-07-01, 2026-08-01]
- month_of_year — Month of year 1-12, year-agnostic — compare the same month across years (e.g. Jan 2025 vs Jan 2026).  [synonyms: month number, month of year, seasonal month; examples: 1, 2, 12]
- week_start — Start date (Monday) of the ISO week — the weekly bucket for trends and week-over-week.  [synonyms: week, week starting, week over week, weekly, week start; examples: 2026-11-09, 2026-11-16]
- iso_week — Week number 1-52, year-agnostic — for comparing the same week across years.  [synonyms: week number, week of year]
- iso_year  [synonyms: year]
- store_id  [synonyms: store, location, fc, store code, franchise code]
- market  [synonyms: market, area, territory; examples: Houston, Dallas, San Antonio]
- region  [synonyms: region, division]
- state  [synonyms: state]
- corporate_franchise  [synonyms: ownership type, corp vs franchise, corporate vs franchise; examples: Corporate, Franchise]
- lifecycle_stage  [synonyms: store status, lifecycle, operational status; examples: Open, Temporarily Closed, Terminated]

METRICS (already aggregated — select by name):
- total_net_sales  [synonyms: sales, net sales, revenue, total sales, product sales; unit: usd]
- units_sold  [synonyms: units, quantity sold, items sold, volume; unit: count]
- traffic  [synonyms: orders, order count, checks, transactions, traffic; unit: count]
- sales_goal — The company's sales goal — the planned/target net sales for a store, per day.
  [synonyms: sales goal, goal, goals, target, sales target, budget, budgeted sales, plan; unit: usd]

RULES:
- SELECT only dimension and metric columns by name; GROUP BY every dimension you select alongside a metric.
- WHERE compares a dimension to a literal with =, !=, <, <=, >, >=, IN, NOT IN, or LIKE. Combine conditions with AND.
- Relative time: use last_period(N, 'day'|'week'|'month') on a date dimension, e.g. `WHERE <date_dim> >= last_period(6, 'week')`. It resolves to the last N COMPLETE <unit>s up to today (the previous N periods, excluding the current partial one) — never write today's date, a literal date range, or guessed week/month numbers.
- Calendar-to-date: for 'year/quarter/month/week to date' (YTD/QTD/MTD, the current period so far, through today) use period_to_date('year'|'quarter'|'month'|'week'), e.g. `WHERE <date_dim> >= period_to_date('year')` for YTD. Use last_period for a trailing window; period_to_date for the current period.
- Filter aggregated measures with HAVING (e.g. `HAVING <metric> > 1000`).
- Rank with `ORDER BY <col> [DESC]` and `LIMIT N`.
- For 'week over week' or a trend over many periods, SELECT the time dimension plus any category so it renders as a line (do NOT pivot).
- To compare a metric across a FEW named periods side by side (e.g. 2025 vs 2026), write a CASE pivot: one `AGG(CASE WHEN <period_dim> = <value> THEN <metric> END)` column per period, grouped by the row bucket — it renders as a grouped bar. Use the metric's own aggregate (SUM for a sum metric).

EXAMPLES:
Q: How is Cappuccino performing week over week?
SQL: SELECT week_start, total_net_sales, units_sold FROM product_sales WHERE product_name = 'Cappuccino' GROUP BY week_start ORDER BY week_start
Q: Net sales by market in the last 30 days
SQL: SELECT market, total_net_sales FROM product_sales WHERE date >= last_period(30, 'day') GROUP BY market
Q: Sales goal vs actual by store
SQL: SELECT store_id, total_net_sales, sales_goal FROM product_sales GROUP BY store_id
Q: What is the week-over-week percent change in Cappuccino net sales?
SQL: SELECT week_start, total_net_sales, 100.0 * (total_net_sales - LAG(total_net_sales) OVER (ORDER BY week_start)) / LAG(total_net_sales) OVER (ORDER BY week_start) AS pct_change FROM product_sales WHERE product_name = 'Cappuccino' GROUP BY week_start ORDER BY week_start
Q: Net sales by month over the last 3 months
SQL: SELECT month, total_net_sales FROM product_sales WHERE date >= last_period(3, 'month') GROUP BY month ORDER BY month
Q: Compare net sales by month for 2025 versus 2026
SQL: SELECT month_of_year, SUM(CASE WHEN iso_year = 2025 THEN total_net_sales END) AS net_sales_2025, SUM(CASE WHEN iso_year = 2026 THEN total_net_sales END) AS net_sales_2026 FROM product_sales GROUP BY month_of_year ORDER BY month_of_year
slides.caption: Emitted by build_system_prompt(model): the LLM's rulebook — it describes the one virtual table (your metrics and dimensions) it may SELECT from. Sent as the system message; the question is the only user input.

slides.title: Validation is the safety boundary
slides.caption: If the SQL references anything not in the model — or isn't a read over the one virtual table — it's rejected (semantic_sql.py + validator.py), and the engine re-plans with the error in a bounded repair loop.

slides.title: The compiler: IR → physical SQL, deterministically
slides.caption: Each IR field maps to a fixed SQL clause. The fan-out guard aggregates each fact table in its own subquery before joining — so a budget row is never multiplied by raw sales lines.

slides.title: One model, any database: cross-dialect portability
slides.caption: The same IR compiles through a small Dialect seam (engine/dialects/) to each engine's SQL — so the product runs on a client's own Postgres or MySQL, unchanged. Data never leaves their infrastructure.

slides.title: Anatomy of the product_sales semantic model
slides.caption: One models/sales.yml: 3 tables joined by 2 relationships expose 3 facts, 15 dimensions and 4 metrics; 6 verified queries teach and test the planner. (Mirrors a Snowflake semantic view.)

slides.title: Synonyms: many phrasings → one canonical name
slides.caption: Every metric and dimension lists its synonyms, so the planner resolves free wording to the one canonical name the schema, compiler and SQL ever see — what makes NL → SQL reliable.

slides.title: Answers, not just tables: deterministic charts + storytelling
slides.caption: The chart is chosen deterministically from the query shape, then a narrate layer adds the takeaway — no LLM in the visual, so it can't mislead. Portable Vega-Lite specs render the same in the app and a future React front end.

slides.title: You can measure it: observability + evals
left: Every turn logged to a local traces.db

slides.title: The LLM proposes. Tested code disposes.
